clean_story:
False signals. Survival’s sleight of hand.

"Deception" in nature means tricking others to live longer—no lies, just built-in misdirection. Like a magician’s trick, but with feathers, chemicals, or flashes of color.

A hoverfly’s yellow stripes mimic a bee’s warning: "I sting." Predators hesitate—though the fly has no stinger. Orchids exploit wasps by smelling like female wasps; males "mate" with petals, pollinating flowers instead. The Amazonian Doodlebug ant draws predators to fake nest entrances, buying time for real tunnels. Even plants deceive: The Passionflower grows fake eggs on leaves, fooling butterflies into laying elsewhere.

Deception isn’t cheating—it’s an arms race. The better the trick, the sharper the senses it forces others to evolve. A cuckoo chick shoves host eggs from the nest; host birds now count eggs like a ledger.

Three related subjects are animal camouflage tricks, plant chemical warfare, and how parasites hijack hosts.
